title: Plumber’s Algebra Structure on Symplectic Cohomology
speaker: Yash Deshmukh
abstract: I will introduce a new structure on (relative) Symplectic Cohomology defined in terms of a PROP called the “Plumber’s PROP.” This PROP consists of nodal Riemann surfaces, of all genera and with multiple inputs and outputs, satisfying a condition that ensures the existence of positive Floer data on the surfaces. This action is defined on the chain-level and generalizes the work of Abouzaid–Groman–Varolgunes. I will discuss the relationship of this structure to cohomological field theories, with potential applications to curve counts, as well as algebraic structures defined on variants of Symplectic Cohomology such as Rabinowitz Floer Cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Motivation from local-to-global Gromov-Witten computation
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames Plumber algebra as a new structure on symplectic cohomology meant to give local-to-global leverage on quantum and Gromov-Witten curve counts, after fixing Novikov coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Relative symplectic cohomology, restriction, descent, and product
proof_stages.summary: Relative symplectic cohomology SH_X(K) is introduced for compact subsets K, with functorial restriction maps, exact/Liouville specializations, Mayer-Vietoris and Čech descent, and a chain-level ring/product descent statement.

proof_stages.label: Gap between compactified Gromov-Witten operations and framed symplectic operations
proof_stages.summary: Higher Gromov-Witten operations are formulated using insertions from Deligne-Mumford compactifications, while existing framed operations on symplectic cohomology use smooth cylindrical-end domains, creating the need for a geometric way to incorporate nodal degenerations.

proof_stages.label: Plumber moduli spaces as controlled nodal domains
proof_stages.summary: Stable curves with cylindrical ends are defined, the Plumber condition is imposed, and the resulting moduli spaces are positioned between smooth framed moduli and full compactified moduli, with more outputs allowing more nodal degenerations.

proof_stages.label: Plumber PROP from gluing cylindrical curves
proof_stages.summary: Chains on possibly disconnected Plumber moduli spaces are assembled into a PROP whose composition is gluing along cylindrical ends and whose tensor product is disjoint union, with broken and related PROPs noted for the actual Floer-theoretic model.

proof_stages.label: Split algebras over PROPs and induced field-theory operations
proof_stages.summary: An algebra over a PROP is defined as a symmetric monoidal functor to chain complexes, and a split Plumber algebra is explained as a chain-level package whose homology yields TFT-type operations indexed by homology classes of Plumber moduli spaces.

proof_stages.label: Existence and compatibility theorems for Plumber algebras
proof_stages.summary: The main results construct split Plumber algebras computing relative symplectic cohomology, make restriction maps algebraic, lift to the base ring in exact cases, and prove compatibility with framed operations, Viterbo-style TCFT structures, and closed-target Gromov-Witten operations.

proof_stages.label: Single-output descent and computational leverage
proof_stages.summary: Although full Plumber-PROP descent is not yet known, descent holds for the single-output substructure, giving a mechanism for local-to-global reductions in favorable computations.

examples.name: Good cover by codimension-zero domains with disjoint boundaries
examples.note: This class of covers is mentioned only to make the good-pair hypothesis for Mayer-Vietoris/Čech descent concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites such covers as cases where the Mayer–Vietoris/descent sequence applies, without working through a particular cover.

examples.name: Cartoon Plumber-condition curves
examples.note: The pictured reducible cylindrical curves show an allowed component with an output, a forbidden component where an input is trapped, and an allowed closed bubble, thereby making the Plumber condition and its name concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: He inspects the cartoon curves component by component to decide whether each satisfies the Plumber condition.

passes.reason: The cartoons are reducible/nodal stable cylindrical curves, including a component with an input and no output used as the non-Plumber failing case.
The cartoon Plumber-condition curves are nodal/reducible boundary degenerations used to illustrate the condition rather than to refute a claim.
the reducible nodal cylindrical cartoons are boundary degenerations, with the stuck-input curve serving as a nonexample to the Plumber condition

examples.name: Higher-genus single-output nodal curve
examples.note: After explaining that genus-zero single-output Plumber spaces see no nodal degenerations, the speaker uses a higher-genus single-output picture to illustrate that this failure is special to genus zero.
examples.engagement_reason: He points to a higher-genus single-output nodal example as showing that more degenerations occur, but does not analyze it in detail.

passes.reason: The higher-genus single-output example is a nodal degeneration in the boundary of the curve moduli space, used to show Plumber spaces include more than smooth curves rather than to refute a claim.

The higher-genus single-output nodal curve is a boundary degeneration at the minimal-output edge and is used to show that the genus-zero single-output smoothness phenomenon does not extend to higher genus.
a nodal higher-genus one-output curve lies on the boundary of moduli and shows the genus-zero smoothness phenomenon does not persist
It is a nodal boundary degeneration in the minimal one-output case, used to show that the genus-zero no-nodes phenomenon fails in higher genus.

A nodal higher-genus stable curve is a degenerate boundary object, and with only one output it lies at the minimal-output/worst-case end while showing the genus-zero single-output phenomenon does not generalize.
A higher-genus single-output nodal boundary curve shows that the genus-zero single-output smoothness phenomenon does not generalize.
It is a nodal boundary curve in the minimal single-output case, shown to defeat the genus-zero intuition that single-output Plumber spaces see no nodal degenerations.
A nodal boundary curve in the minimal single-output case is used to show the genus-zero no-degeneration phenomenon fails in higher genus.
A nodal higher-genus curve in the minimal single-output case shows that the genus-zero single-output collapse to smooth curves does not generalize.
The nodal higher-genus curve is a boundary degeneration in the minimal single-output case and shows that the genus-zero no-new-nodes phenomenon does not extend to higher genus.
A one-output higher-genus nodal boundary degeneration refutes the genus-zero-only behavior.
The higher-genus one-output nodal curve shows that the genus-zero one-output no-nodes phenomenon does not extend to higher genus, and it is a nodal boundary/minimal-output case.

examples.name: Genus-one Gromov-Witten operation on a symplectic two-torus
examples.note: The torus is covered by two half-cylinders so that a degree-one class vanishes on each piece, illustrating how single-output descent can reduce insertion degree and turn the computation into a simpler unit/class calculation.
examples.engagement_reason: He sets up the genus-one operation on the two-torus and outlines a concrete local-to-global reduction using a half-cylinder cover and vanishing restrictions of the class.

passes.reason: The genus-one Gromov–Witten operation on a symplectic two-torus is a computation exploiting the torus’s translation symmetries, not an obstruction or sporadic object.
